title: Introduction and Research Context
objective: Introduce the research domain of pedestrian crossing behavior, establish the importance of understanding both intentional violations and distraction effects, and outline the scope of this review.

title: Theoretical Foundations of Pedestrian Crossing Behavior
objective: Present the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) framework as applied to pedestrian violating behavior, and establish the psychological constructs (attitudes, norms, perceived control) that inform behavioral prediction models.

title: Behavioral Factors Influencing Crossing Intentions
objective: Synthesize evidence on key psychological predictors of pedestrian violating behavior, including instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency, and evaluate their relative importance in structural models.

title: The Role of Perceived Risk and Control in Crossing Behavior
objective: Examine evidence on why perceived behavioral control and perceived risk often fail to predict pedestrian crossing violations, and discuss implications for safety interventions.

title: Mobile Phone Distraction Effects on Crossing Performance
objective: Synthesize experimental findings on how different types of mobile phone use (texting, conversation, music) differentially impair pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation.

title: Visual Attention Allocation Under Distraction
objective: Analyze eye-tracking evidence on how mobile phone distraction affects pedestrians' visual scanning patterns, fixation points, and attention to traffic environment.

title: Environmental and Design Factors in Pedestrian Safety
objective: Integrate findings on how infrastructure design, traffic conditions, and environmental factors interact with behavioral and distraction effects in pedestrian crossing safety.

title: Methodological Approaches and Their Contributions
objective: Compare the strengths and limitations of structural equation modeling approaches versus controlled experimental eye-tracking studies for understanding pedestrian crossing behavior.

title: Conclusions and Future Research Directions
objective: Synthesize the key findings across behavioral prediction and distraction research, identify convergent themes, and propose directions for future investigation.
